content:
# Research: AI Denoising Models for Underwater Video (RTX 3060 6GB VRAM)

## Context
Dorea pipeline needs a denoising stage for underwater video footage.
Hardware: RTX 3060, 6GB VRAM, sequential GPU scheduling (denoiser runs alone, up to ~5GB VRAM available).
Problem: sensor/ISO noise, chroma noise, fine grain in low-light underwater footage.

## Candidate Models — Summary

### 1. NAFNet (Nonlinear Activation Free Network)
- Paper: ECCV 2022 (megvii-research); actively maintained
- Architecture: Plain CNN encoder-decoder (UNet-like), no nonlinear activations
- Variants: width32 (~17M params), width64 (~67M params)
- SIDD benchmark: 40.30 dB PSNR — state-of-art at release
- VRAM: width32 fits ~1–1.5GB at 1080p; width64 ~2–3GB; tiling available for 4K
- Video: Image-only (no temporal). Use with TAP (see below) for video.
- Chroma: Yes — trained on SIDD (full-color sensor noise), handles both luma and chroma
- Weights: Official pretrained (Google Drive / Hugging Face), Apache 2.0
- GitHub: https://github.com/megvii-research/NAFNet
- Verdict: Best image-denoising baseline for Dorea. Fast, lightweight, excellent real-noise quality.

### 2. TAP — Temporal As a Plugin (ECCV 2024)
- Paper: ECCV 2024 (zfu006)
- Architecture: Plugs temporal modules into a frozen NAFNet backbone (unsupervised)
- Video: YES — adds temporal consistency on top of NAFNet without retraining the image denoiser
- VRAM: ~NAFNet width64 + small overhead (~3–4GB at 1080p)
- Chroma: Inherits NAFNet's full-color handling
- Weights: Available (GitHub), based on KAIR/RViDeNet/NAFNet
- GitHub: https://github.com/zfu006/TAP
- Verdict: Strong practical choice — combines NAFNet quality with temporal consistency. Ideal for video.

### 3. Restormer (CVPR 2022, Oral)
- Architecture: Transformer with MDTA (Multi-Dconv Head Transposed Attention) + GDFN
- ~26M parameters
- VRAM: ~2–3GB at 1080p (processes in tiles at high res)
- Video: Image-only (no temporal)
- Chroma: YES — has real-image denoising weights (SIDD + DND), full-color
- Weights: Official, Google Drive, free for research
- GitHub: https://github.com/swz30/Restormer
- Verdict: Slightly higher quality than NAFNet on some benchmarks but slower. Good fallback.

### 4. SCUNet (Swin-Conv-UNet, 2023)
- Paper: Machine Intelligence Research 2023 (cszn/Kaizhang)
- Architecture: Hybrid Swin Transformer + CNN UNet blocks
- VRAM: Moderate (~2–3GB at 1080p); inference ~3s/frame on T4
- Video: Image-only; no temporal module
- Chroma: YES — blind real denoising, handles color noise
- Weights: Official (color + grayscale), free
- GitHub: https://github.com/cszn/SCUNet
- Verdict: Strong real-world blind denoiser. Slower than NAFNet but good for complex noise.

### 5. RVRT (Recurrent Video Restoration Transformer, NeurIPS 2022)
- Architecture: Recurrent transformer with guided deformable attention, processes video clips
- ~13M parameters (denoising variant)
- VRAM: Trained on 8x A100 (29GB total); at inference, tiling supported; expect ~3–5GB for 1080p clips
- Video: YES — native video model, strong temporal consistency
- Chroma: YES — trained on DAVIS (color video)
- Weights: Official pretrained, free for research
- GitHub: https://github.com/JingyunLiang/RVRT
- Verdict: Best pure-video denoiser. More VRAM-hungry than NAFNet+TAP but native temporal modeling.

### 6. FastDVDnet (CVPR 2020)
- Architecture: Two stacked U-Nets, 5-frame sliding window, no optical flow
- ~1.3M parameters
- VRAM: Very low (~500MB at 1080p)
- Video: YES — real-time capable on modern GPU
- Chroma: Partial — trained on Gaussian noise; real chroma noise handling weaker than RVRT/NAFNet
- Weights: Official, MIT license
- GitHub: https://github.com/m-tassano/fastdvdnet
- Verdict: Fastest option; use only if speed is critical and noise is not severe chroma noise. Quality lags behind 2023+ models.

### 7. VRT (Video Restoration Transformer)
- Architecture: Multi-scale temporal mutual self-attention transformer
- ~35M parameters
- VRAM: HIGH — impractical on 6GB VRAM for 1080p without heavy tiling
- Video: YES
- Verdict: Too large for RTX 3060 without significant tiling overhead; RVRT is the better recurrent alternative.

### 8. RealESRGAN (denoising mode)
- Primary purpose: Super-resolution; incidental denoising via degradation model
- Denoising strength: --dn 0–1 flag (realesr-general-x4v3 model)
- VRAM: ~2–3GB with tiling
- Video: No native temporal; flickering expected without post-processing
- Chroma: Partial — real degradation model includes color noise
- Verdict: Not recommended as primary denoiser — upscaling artifacts if used at 1x scale. Only if pipeline also wants SR.
- GitHub: https://github.com/xinntao/Real-ESRGAN

### 9. BSRGAN / DnCNN
- DnCNN: Classic 2017 CNN; Gaussian noise only; NOT suitable for real chroma noise
- BSRGAN: Degradation model for SR; not a video denoiser
- Verdict: Skip for this use case.

### 10. Blind2Unblind / Noise2Fast (self-supervised)
- Use case: When no clean reference data is available and noise model is unknown
- VRAM: Moderate
- Video: Image-based; no temporal
- Verdict: Overkill for Dorea — NAFNet is trained on real sensor noise (SIDD) which already covers underwater ISO noise. Use self-supervised only if domain shift is extreme.

### 11. RViDeformer (TCSVT 2025)
- Architecture: Multi-branch spatial+temporal attention, raw video denoising
- Dataset: ReCRVD (ISO 1600–25600 raw video)
- Note: Operates on RAW Bayer frames (pre-ISP), not processed video
- Verdict: Interesting but requires RAW input. Underwater footage is typically processed H.264/H.265.

### 12. RFCVD / LEVD — "Classic Video Denoising in a ML World" (CVPR 2025)
- Architecture: Differentiable traditional pipeline (Wiener + bilateral Laplacian pyramid) parameterized by a neural network noise profiler
- Separate luma/chroma denoising channels — explicit chroma noise control
- Fast, robust, controllable — designed to avoid deep learning distribution mismatch
- Project: https://srameo.github.io/projects/levd/
- Verdict: Highly relevant for underwater footage where noise model may not match SIDD training data. Watch for code release.

## Recommended Pipeline for Dorea

**Recommended combination**: NAFNet-SIDD-width64 + TAP temporal plugin
- Quality: SOTA real-noise denoising (SIDD-trained = camera sensor noise = matches underwater ISO noise)
- Temporal: TAP adds flicker-free temporal consistency
- VRAM: ~3–4GB, well within 6GB budget
- Chroma: Full RGB — handles both luma and chroma noise
- Speed: NAFNet is fast (sub-second per frame at 1080p)
- License: Apache 2.0 / research use

**Fallback if chroma noise is dominant**: SCUNet (blind denoising, color-aware)
**Fallback if speed is critical**: FastDVDnet (very low VRAM, real-time capable)
**Watch**: RFCVD/LEVD (CVPR 2025) for explicit chroma control and robustness to out-of-distribution noise

## Pipeline Integration Notes
- Run denoising BEFORE color grading (LUT application) in the Dorea pipeline
- Insert as a new phase (e.g., Phase 0.5 or Phase 1.5) after frame extraction
- Use fp16 inference to halve VRAM consumption
- Tiling (e.g., 256 or 512px overlap) for 4K frames
- Unload model and call torch.cuda.empty_cache() before SAM2 (Phase 3)
